سمندر.e: Semi-learned borrowing from Sanskrit समुद्र (samudra). Doublet of سَمُدْر (samudr). First attested in c. 1564 as Middle Hindi سمندر (smndr).

senses.g: Samundri (a city in Faisalabad district, Punjab, Pakistan)

سموسہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سنبوسه (sanbōsa), from Middle Persian.

سمین.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سَمِین (samīn), from Arabic سَمِين (samīn).

سن.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سن (sinn), from Arabic سِنّ (sinn).

senses.g: existing from the old, immemorial, continuing, eternal
orthodox Hinduism as opposed to new reform movements such as Arya Samaj

سناتن دھرم.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit सनातन धर्म (sanātana dharma, “the eternal religion”), from सनातन (sanātana, “eternal”) + धर्म (dharma, “law”).

senses.g: absolute silence – complete absence of sound, often connoted with "eerily quiet".
the effect of a blow; a strike
a heavy; strong sound (of rain etc.)

سنار.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi سنار (sunār) (c. 1609) / سونار (svnar /⁠sūnār⁠/) (c. 1572) , from Apabhramsa 𑆱𑆶𑆤𑇀𑆤𑆳𑆫 (sunnāra), from Prakrit 𑀲𑀼𑀡𑁆𑀡𑀸𑀭 (suṇṇāra) from Sanskrit सुवर्णकार (suvarṇakāra), compound of सुवर्ण (suvárṇa) + कार (kāra).

senses.g: to tell; to cause to listen
to give a piece of one's mind
to support, sustain; to hold up
to hold, to handle, to maintain

سنبھالنا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀲𑀁𑀪𑀸𑀭𑁂𑀇 (saṃbhārei, “garnishes”), 𑀲𑀁𑀪𑀸𑀮𑀺𑀅 (saṃbhālia, “supported”), from Sanskrit संभारयति (saṃbhārayati, “causes to bring together, prepares, maintains”).

senses.g: circumcision (excising the foreskin from the penis)
qurbani; the sacrifice of livestock on Eid ul-Adha

سنت ابراہیمی.e: Ezafe compound of سُنَّت (sunnat, “Sunnah; practice; tradition”) + ِ (-i, “of”) + اِبْرَاہِیمِی (ibrāhīmī, “Abrahamic; relating to Abraham”), literally “the tradition of Abraham”.

سنترا.e: Borrowed from Portuguese Sintra (“a town in Lisboa, Portugal”). Compare semantically Italian portogallo (“sweet orange”, literally “Portugal”) and Gujarati સંતરું (santrũ).

سنجیدہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سنجیده (sanjida) (compare سنجیدن (sanjidan, “to ponder, reflect”)), from Proto-Iranian *θanǰáyati, from Proto-Indo-European *t(ʰ)ongʰ-éye-ti, from *t(ʰ)engʰ- (“to draw, pull back”), perhaps an extension of *ten-.

سندر.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit सुन्दर (sundara). First attested in c. 1611 as Middle Hindi سندر (sndr /⁠sundar⁠/).

senses.g: Indus (a river in the provinces of Sindh, Punjab and Khyber Pakhtunkhwa and the administrative territory of Gilgit-Baltistan in Pakistan, the union territory of Ladakh in India and the autonomous region of Tibet in China)

ex.u: دَرْیَائے سِنْدھ
ex.r: daryā'e sindh
ex.e: The Indus river

senses.g: Sind, Scinde (a province of British India)
the cycle of birth and death

سنسان.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit *𑀲𑀼𑀡𑁆𑀡𑀲𑀸𑀡 (*suṇṇasāṇa), from Sanskrit शून्यस्थान n (śūnyasthāna, “empty place”), from शून्य (śūnya, “empty”) + स्थान (sthāna, “place”). Also see سُونا (sūnā) and تھانَہ (thāna).

senses.g: a compound with a stone wall
Singapore (an island and city-state in Southeast Asia, located off the southernmost tip of the Malay Peninsula; a former British crown colony and state of Malaysia (1963-1965))
adornment, embellishment, ornament (flowers, jewellery, clothes, etc.)

سنگار.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀲𑀺𑀁𑀕𑀸𑀭 (siṃgāra), from Sanskrit शृङ्गार (śṛṅgāra). Cognate with Punjabi سِن٘گار (siṉgār).

ex.u: یا الہ آباد میں رہیے جہاں سنگم بھی ہو یا بنارس میں جہاں ہر گھاٹ پر سیلاب ہے
ex.r: yā ilāhābād mẽ rahie jahā̃ saṅgam bhī ho yā banāras mẽ jahā̃ har ghāṭ par sailāb hai
ex.e: Stay either in Allahabad where there is confluence, or in Varanasi where there is flood at every pier.

senses.g: confluence, junction — the confluence of the Ganges and Yamuna at Allahabad
the Sangam period of ancient Tamil Nadu

سنگھرش.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit संघर्ष (saṃgharṣa, “friction; rivalry”).

سننا.e: Urdu verb set سُنْنا (sunnā) سُنانا (sunānā) سُنْوانا (sunvānā) Inherited from Sanskrit शृणोति (śṛṇoti), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *ćr̥náwti, from Proto-Indo-European *ḱl̥néwti, from *ḱlew- + *-né-.

سنیچر.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi سنیچر (sanīcar) (c. 1695), from Prakrit 𑀲𑀡𑀺𑀘𑁆𑀘𑀭 (saṇiccara), likely from Sanskrit *शनैश्चर (*śanaiścara), from शनि (śani) + चर (cara). Cognate with Awadhi and Bhojpuri सनीचर (sanīcar), Punjabi ਛਨਿੱਛਰ /چَھنِچَّھر (chanicchar), Sindhi ڇَن٘ڇَرُ (chañcharu).

senses.g: poverty; dirt; dirty clothes — a miser or stingy / gluttonous person

سنیچر.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi سنیچر (sanīcar) (c. 1695), from Prakrit 𑀲𑀡𑀺𑀘𑁆𑀘𑀭 (saṇiccara), likely from Sanskrit *शनैश्चर (*śanaiścara), from शनि (śani) + चर (cara). Cognate with Awadhi and Bhojpuri सनीचर (sanīcar), Punjabi ਛਨਿੱਛਰ /چَھنِچَّھر (chanicchar), Sindhi ڇَن٘ڇَرُ (chañcharu).

سو.e: Inherited from Sanskrit शत (śata), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *ćatám, from Proto-Indo-European *ḱm̥tóm. Compare also Persian صد (sad).

سوار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سوار (sawār, “rider”), from Old Persian 𐎠𐎿𐎲𐎠𐎼 (a-s-b-a-r /⁠asabāra-⁠/, “horseman”), from Proto-Iranian *Hacwabāráh, from Proto-Indo-Iranian *Haćwabʰāras.

سوال.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سَوَال (sawāl, variant form of سُؤَال (su'āl)), borrowed from Arabic سُؤَال (suʔāl).

senses.g: a part in a play, a character, role

سوانگ.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit *𑀲𑀯𑀸𑀁𑀕 (*savāṃga), from Sanskrit *समाङ्ग (*samāṅga), from सम (samá) + अङ्ग (áṅga). Cognate with Marathi सोंग (soṅga), Gujarati સોંગ (soṅg), સ્વાંગ (svā̃g), Punjabi ਸਾਂਗ (sāṅg) / سانگ (sāng), ਸ੍ਵਾਂਗ (svāṅg), ਸਵਾਂਗ (savāṅg) / سوانگ (soāng), Odia ସୁଆଙ୍ଗ (suāṅga).

سوپن.e: From Sanskrit स्वप्न (svápna), ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *swépnos, from the root *swep- (“to sleep”).

سوتا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀲𑁄𑀢𑁆𑀢 (sotta), from Sanskrit *स्रोत्र (*srotra), from स्रु (sru, “to flow”).

senses.g: inflection of سونا (sonā, “to sleep”): — masculine singular habitual participle
inflection of سونا (sonā, “to sleep”): — masculine singular contrafactual

سوتن.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀲𑀯𑀢𑁆𑀢𑀺𑀡𑀻 (savattiṇī). Doublet of سَوت (saut) and سَوکَن (saukan).

senses.g: from a different wife, from a co-wife
direct plural/ oblique singular/ vocative singular of سوتا (sotā)
inflection of سونا (sonā, “to sleep”): — masculine plural habitual participle
inflection of سونا (sonā, “to sleep”): — oblique masculine singular habitual participle
inflection of سونا (sonā, “to sleep”): — masculine plural contrafactual
alternative form of سوٹ کیس (soṭ kes)

سوجن.e: From سُوجْنا (sūjnā, “to swell”), from Sanskrit शूयते (śūyatē).

سوچنا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀲𑁄𑀘𑁆𑀘𑀤𑀺 (soccadi, “to purify”), from Sanskrit शोच्यते (śocyate, “to be purified; to be made to feel pain”), from the root शुच् (śuc). Cognate with Punjabi سوچنا / ਸੋਚਣਾ (socṇā), Sindhi سوچڻ (socṇ) / सोचणु (socaṇu), Nepali सोच्नु (socnu), Assamese সুচা (xusa), Odia ଶୋଚିବା (śocibā, “to grieve”).

senses.g: goods, wares; trade, traffic; marketing; purchase, bargain
the black bile (one of the four humours of the body), atrabilis; melancholy; hypochondria; frenzy, madness, insanity; love; desire, concupiscence; ambition
Sudan (a country in North Africa and East Africa)

سؤر.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀲𑀽𑀕𑀭 (sūgara), *𑀲𑀽𑀅𑀭 (*sūara), from Sanskrit सूकर (sūkara), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *suHkarás, from Proto-Indo-European *suh₂kéh₂ (“pig, swine”). Compare Punjabi سُوْر (sūr), Kashmiri سور / सोर (sōr).

senses.g: Surab (a city in Surab district, Balochistan, Pakistan)
Surankote, Surankot (a town, a tehsil in Poonch district, Jammu and Kashmir, India)
alternative form of سورت (sūrat, “chapter of the Qur'an”)
synonym of شام (śām): Syria (a country in West Asia in the Middle East)

سوریہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سُورِیَه (sūriya), from Arabic سُورِيَة (sūriya).

سوستک.e: From Sanskrit स्वस्तिक (svástika, “a lucky or auspicious object”).

senses.g: a rarity, souvenir, gift (brought back from travelling)

ex.u: اِس کے باوجُود اس کے نغمے سوغاتوں کی طرح سفر کرتے تھے۔
ex.r: Faizan-e-Faiz
ex.e: Despite this, his melodies used to travel like souvenirs.

سوکھا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀲𑀼𑀓𑁆𑀔 (sukkha) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-), from Sanskrit शुष्क (śuṣka). Doublet of خُشْک (xuśk).

ex.u: جاؤ دھوپ میں کپڑے ڈال آؤ، کپڑا سوکھنے میں بھی تو ٹائم لگے گا
ex.e: Go and put the clothes out in the sun, it’ll take time for them to dry too.

ex.u: تم کتنے سوکھ گئے ہو
ex.e: You’ve really thinned out!

سوکھنا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀲𑀼𑀓𑁆𑀔𑀇 (sukkhaï), from Sanskrit शुष्यति (śuṣyati). From the root शुष् (śuṣ).
Inherited from Old Hindi सोषना (soṣanā), Semi-learned borrowing from Sanskrit शोषयति (śoṣayati). From the root शुष् (śuṣ).

سوگ.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀲𑁄𑀕 (soga), from Sanskrit शोक (śoka). Also influenced by Classical Persian سوگ (sōg). Doublet of شوک (śok).

سونا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀲𑀼𑀡𑁆𑀡 (suṇṇa), from Sanskrit शून्य (śūnya, “empty”). Compare Punjabi سنجا / ਸੁੰਞਾ (suñā), Nepali सुन् (sun), Gujarati સૂનું (sūnũ), Marathi सुना (sunā). Related to سُنْسان (sunsān).

Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀲𑀼𑀡𑁆𑀡 (suṇṇa), from Sanskrit शून्य (śūnya, “empty”). Compare Punjabi سنجا / ਸੁੰਞਾ (suñā), Nepali सुन् (sun), Gujarati સૂનું (sūnũ), Marathi सुना (sunā). Related to سُنْسان (sunsān).

Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀲𑁄𑀡𑀅𑀁 (soṇaaṃ), from Sanskrit सुवर्ण (suvarṇa, “golden”). Compare Marathi सोने (sone), ਸੋਨਾ (sonā), Nepali सुन (suna), Gujarati સોનું (sonũ), Odia ସୁନା (sunā), Bengali সোনা (śōna), Assamese সোণ (xün)/সণা (xona).

ex.u: میں رات کو دس بجے سوتا ہوں۔
ex.r: ma͠i rāt ko das baje sotā hū̃
ex.e: I sleep at ten o'clock at night.

ex.u: میرا بھائی پڑھتے پڑھتے سو گیا۔
ex.r: mēra bhāī paṛhte-paṛhte so gayā
ex.e: My brother fell asleep while reading.

سونا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit [Term?], from Sanskrit/Vedic Sanskrit स्वपति (svapati), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *swápati, from Proto-Indo-European *swépeti. Cognate with Nepali सुत्नु (sutnu).

سونگھنا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀲𑀺𑀁𑀖𑀤𑀺 (siṃghadi), 𑀲𑀼𑀁𑀖𑀤𑀺 (suṃghadi), from Sanskrit *शृङ्खति (*śṛṅkhati, “to smell”), from the root *शृङ्ख् (*śṛṅkh). Compare Sanskrit शिङ्घति (śiṅghati), Punjabi ਸੁੰਘਣਾ (suṅghṇā) / سنگھنا, Gujarati સૂંઘવું (sū̃ghvũ).

سوہن.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi سوہن (svhn /⁠sohan⁠/) (c. 1611), from Prakrit 𑀲𑁄𑀳𑀡 (sohaṇa), from Sanskrit शोभन (śobhana). Cognates Cognate with Punjabi سوہْݨا (sohṇā) / ਸੋਹਣਾ (sohṇā), Pahari-Potwari سوہݨا (sohṇā), Saraiki سوݨھا (soṇhā), Sindhi سُهَڻو (suhaṇo), Domaaki شون (šōna), Gujarati સોહણી (sohṇī), and Assamese শুৱনি (xuni).

Inherited from Middle Hindi سوہن (svhn /⁠sohan⁠/) (c. 1611), from Prakrit 𑀲𑁄𑀳𑀡 (sohaṇa), from Sanskrit शोभन (śobhana). Cognates Cognate with Punjabi سوہْݨا (sohṇā) / ਸੋਹਣਾ (sohṇā), Pahari-Potwari سوہݨا (sohṇā), Saraiki سوݨھا (soṇhā), Sindhi سُهَڻو (suhaṇo), Domaaki شون (šōna), Gujarati સોહણી (sohṇī), and Assamese শুৱনি (xuni).

senses.g: Switzerland (a country in Western Europe and Central Europe)
Sweden (a country in Scandinavia in Northern Europe)

سویرا.e: Inherited from Sanskrit *सवेल (*savela). Cognate with Bhojpuri सबेर (sabēr).

سوئی.e: Inherited from Old Hindi सुई (suī), सूई (sūī), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀲𑀽𑀈 (sūī), from Sanskrit सूची (sūcī́).

senses.g: Sui (a city in Dera Bugti district, Balochistan, Pakistan)

سوئی.e: Inherited from Old Hindi सुई (suī), सूई (sūī), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀲𑀽𑀈 (sūī), from Sanskrit सूची (sūcī́).

senses.g: a selection of marriage selection contest for a bride

سہارا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀲𑀳𑀸𑀭 (sahāra), 𑀲𑀳𑀬𑀸𑀭 (sahayāra) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-), from Sanskrit सहकार (sahakāra).

senses.g: wifehood (ie. wedlock; marriage; wedding – period of being married)
ornaments and things which are worn in the state of being married.

سہاگ.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀲𑁄𑀳𑀕𑁆𑀕 (sohagga), from Sanskrit सौभाग्य (saubhāgya). First attested in c. 1611 as Middle Hindi سُہاگ (shag) / سُوہاگ (svhag).

ex.u: اِس دُنْیا میں مومِنوں کے لِیے صِرْف اَللّٰہ ہِی سَہایَک ہے
ex.r: is dunyā mẽ mominõ ke liye sirf allāh hī sahāyak he
ex.e: Indeed Allah is the only helper of believers in this world.

سہس.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi سهس (shs /⁠sahas⁠/), from Old Hindi सहस (sahasa), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀲𑀳𑀲𑁆𑀲 (sahassa), from Ashokan Prakrit 𑀲𑀳𑀲𑁆𑀭 (sahasra), from Sanskrit सहस्र (sahásra). Doublet of ہَزار (hazār). Displaced by ہَزار (hazār).

سہسرابدی.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit सहस्राब्दी (sahasrābdī). By surface analysis, سَہَسْرَابْد (sahasrābd) + ـِی (-ī).

سہم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سهم (sahm), ultimately from Proto-Iranian *θráhma.

سہمانا.e: Causative form of سہمنا (sahamnā) taking the suffix ـانا

ex.u: ہم سب سہمت ہے.
ex.e: We all do agree.

سہمت.e: Sanskrit सह (saha, “together, along”) + मत (mata, “opinion”). Possibly related to سَمَّت (sammat).

سہمنا.e: From Classical Persian سهم (sahm, “fear”) + ـنا (-nā, verbal suffix).

ex.u: اللہ ہمیشہ سَہَن شِیل لوگوں کے ساتھ ہے.
ex.e: Allah is always with those who have patience.

سہولت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سهولت (suhūlat), from Arabic سُهُولَة (suhūla).

senses.g: Sehwan (a city in Jamshoro district, Sindh, Pakistan)

سہیلی.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi سہیلی (shyly /⁠sahelī⁠/) (c. 1564), from Prakrit 𑀲𑀳𑀻 (sahī) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀮- (-la-) + -𑀇𑀓𑀸- (-ikā-), from Sanskrit सखी (sakhī, “woman's confidante; mistress”), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *sákʰā, ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *sokʷ-yo, from the root *sekʷ- (“to follow”). Doublet of سَکھی (sakhī). Cognate with Punjabi ਸਹੇਲੀ (sahelī) / سَہیلی (sahelī) and Sindhi ساهيڙي (saheṛī).

سیاح.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سَیَّاح (sayyāh), from Arabic سَيَّاح (sayyāḥ).

سیاحت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سیاحت (siyāhat), from Arabic سِيَاحَة (siyāḥa).

سیارہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سیاره (sayyāra), from Arabic سَيَّارَة (sayyāra), itself an ellipsis of كَوْكَب سَيَّار (kawkab sayyār, “wandering star”).

سیاست.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سِیَاسَت (siyāsat), from Arabic سِيَاسَة (siyāsa). Compare Sindhi سياست (syāst).

سیاستدان.e: From سیاست (siyāsat) + ـدان (-dān), both elements from Persian.

سیاسی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سیاسی (siyāsī), itself from Arabic سِيَاسِيّ (siyāsiyy). By surface analysis, سیاست (siyāsat) + ـی (-ī).

senses.g: Sialkot (a city in Sialkot district, Punjab, Pakistan)

سیالکوٹ.e: Inherited from Sanskrit शकल (śakala) + Sanskrit कोट (koṭa, “fort”).

سیاہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سیاه (siyāh), from Avestan 𐬯𐬌𐬌𐬁𐬎𐬎𐬀 (siiāuua), ultimately from Proto-Indo-Iranian *ćyaHwás. Cognate with Sanskrit श्याव (śyāva), Russian сивый (sivyj, “grey”), Lithuanian šývas (“light grey”), Old English hīew (whence English hue).

سیب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سیب (sēb, “apple”), from Proto-Iranian *cáywaH.

senses.g: a female given name, Sita, from Sanskrit, of Hindu usage

سیتا.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit सीता (sītā). First attested in c. 1700 as Middle Hindi سیتا (syta /⁠sītā⁠/).

سیٹھا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀲𑀺𑀝𑁆𑀞 (siṭṭha), from Sanskrit शिष्ट (śiṣṭa, “left over”).

سیدھا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀲𑀺𑀤𑁆𑀥 (siddha), from Sanskrit सिद्ध (siddha).

سیر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سَیر (sayr), from Arabic سَيْر (sayr).

ex.u:
یہ راس رنگ یہ میل ملن اک ہاتھ میں چاند اک میں سورج
اک رات کا موج مزہ سارا اک دن کا سیر سپاٹا ہے
ye rās raṅg ye mel milan ik hāth mẽ cānd ik mẽ sūraj
ex.r: ik rāt kā mauj mazā sārā ik din kā sair sapāṭā hai
ex.e: (please add an English translation of this quotation)

سیرت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سِیرَت (sīrat), from Arabic سِيرَة (sīra).

سیڑھی.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀲𑀺𑀟𑁆𑀠𑀺 (siḍḍhi), from Sanskrit *श्रीढि (*śrīḍhi).

سیکھنا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀲𑀺𑀓𑁆𑀔𑀇 (sikkhaï), from Sanskrit शिक्ष॑ति (śíkṣati), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *ćíkšati, from Proto-Indo-European *ḱí-ḱₔk-se-ti, from *ḱek-.

سینچنا.e: Inherited from Apabhramsa 𑆱𑆴𑆁𑆖𑆼𑆅 (siṃcei), from Prakrit 𑀲𑀺𑀁𑀘𑀇 (siṃcaï), from Sanskrit सिञ्चति (siñcati).

سینکڑا.e: Inherited from Sanskrit शत (śatá, “hundred”) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓𑁆𑀓- (-kka-) + -𑀟- (-ḍa-) + -𑀓- (-ka-). Cognate with Punjabi ਸੈਂਕੜਾ (saiṅkṛā) / سَینکڑا (sainkṛā), Gujarati સેંકડો (seṅkḍo), Marathi शेकडा (śekḍā), शेकडो (śekḍo).

Inherited from Sanskrit शत (śatá, “hundred”) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓𑁆𑀓- (-kka-) + -𑀟- (-ḍa-) + -𑀓- (-ka-). Cognate with Punjabi ਸੈਂਕੜਾ (saiṅkṛā) / سَینکڑا (sainkṛā), Gujarati સેંકડો (seṅkḍo), Marathi शेकडा (śekḍā), शेकडो (śekḍo).

سینگ.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀲𑀺𑀁𑀕 (siṃga), from Sanskrit शृङ्ग (śṛṅga), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *ćr̥Hngám, from earlier *ćr̥Hnám, from Proto-Indo-European *ḱr̥h₂-nó-m, from *ḱerh₂- (“head, horn”). Cognate with Marathi शिंग (śiṅga), Sylheti ꠢꠤꠋ (hiṅ), Assamese শিং (xiṅ).

senses.g: large metal tray used as a low-lying round table
indicates association or mutual dealing: by; with; to

ex.u: وہ روز اپنے پڑوسی سے لڑتا ہے۔
ex.r: vo roz apne paṛosī se laṛtā hai.
ex.e: He fights with his neighbor every day.

senses.g: indicates a sense of removal or going: from — indicates exiting or separation

ex.u: وہ گھر سے نکل کر ڈاک گھر چلا۔
ex.r: vo ghar se nikal kar ḍāk-ghar calā.
ex.e: He left from the house and went to the post office.

senses.g: indicates a sense of removal or going: from — indicates source, cause, reason

ex.u: یہ تلوار دو صدیوں پہلے لوہے سے بنایا گیا تھا۔
ex.r: ye talvār do sadiyõ pahle lohe se banāyā gayā thā.
ex.e: This sword was made from iron two centuries ago.

senses.g: indicates a sense of removal or going: from — indicates origin, beginning

ex.u: میں کراچی سے آیا ہوں۔
ex.r: ma͠i karācī se āyā hū̃.
ex.e: I've come here from Karachi.

ex.u: میں اُس سے ہوشیار ہوں۔
ex.r: ma͠i us se hośiyār hū̃.
ex.e: I'm smarter than him.

ex.u: امی دیر سے یہاں بیٹھی ہوئی ہے۔
ex.r: ammī der se yahā̃ baiṭhī huī hai.
ex.e: Mom has been sitting here for a while.

ex.u: اِس کپڑے کو پانی سے دھونا ہو گا۔
ex.r: is kapṛe ko pānī se dhonā ho gā.
ex.e: This cloth will have to be washed with water.

senses.g: indicates manner of doing; forms adverbs

ex.u: دھیان سے سنیں۔
ex.r: dhyān se sunẽ.
ex.e: Listen carefully.

سے.e: Uncertain. Several etymologies have been proposed, and a merger of some (or even all) of them is extremely likely. * Inherited from Sanskrit समम् (samam, “together; similarly, as”), from सम (samá, “same”), from Proto-Indo-Aryan *samHás, from Proto-Indo-Iranian *samHás, from Proto-Indo-European *somHós (“same”). Compare Braj सौं (sa͠u, “to, with”). * Inherited from Sanskrit सङ्गे (saṅge), locative of सङ्ग (saṅgá, “contact; battle”). Doublet of سَن٘گ (saṅg). * Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀲𑀳𑀺𑀤 (sahida), 𑀲𑀳𑀺𑀅 (sahia), from Sanskrit सहित (sahita, “with; accompanying”), from स- (sa-, “with”) + हित (hita, “placed”).

senses.g: The nineteenth letter of the Urdu alphabet, written in the Arabic script; preceded by س and followed by ص.
A suffix used in many nouns borrowed from Persian, and also in certain native words. This is a rough equivalent of -tion or -ment, making a noun for the action of the verb.
bravo!, excellent!, good job!, well done!

ex.u: پَت جَھڑ کِی وہ شاخ اَبھِی تَک کان٘پ رَہی ہَے / وہ شاخ گِرا دو میرا وہ سامان لَوٹا دو
ex.r: gulzār
ex.e: That branch of autumn still shakes / Let that branch fall, return my belongings

شاخ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شَاخ (šāx). Doublet of شاکھا (śākhā).

ex.u: پاک سرزمین شاد باد
ex.r: pāk sarzamīn śād-bād
ex.e: May the Pak (literally, pure or clean) homeland be happy

شاد باد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شاد باد (šād bād).

شاد باش.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شاد باش (šād-bāš).

شادی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شادی (šâdi). Equivalent to شاد (śād) + ـی (-ī).

senses.g: Sharjah (an emirate of the United Arab Emirates)
Sharjah (the capital city of Sharjah emirate, United Arab Emirates)

شارجہ.e: Borrowed from Gulf Arabic الشارقة (aš-šārja). Likely influenced from English Sharjah and/or Persian شارجه (šârje).

شارع.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شارع (šāri'), from Arabic شَارِع (šāriʕ).
Borrowed from Classical Persian شارع (šāri'), from Arabic شَارِع (šāriʕ).

شاسن.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit शासन (śāsana), from the root verb शास् (śās, “to order, command, direct”).

شاطر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شَاطِر (šātir), from Arabic شَاطِر (šāṭir).

شاعر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شاعر (šā'ir), from Arabic شَاعِر (šāʕir).

شاعرہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شاعره (šā'ira), from Arabic شَاعِرة (šāʕira). By surface analysis, شاعر (śā'ir) + ـه (-a).

شاعری.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شاعری (šā'irī, “art of poetry”). By surface analysis, شاعر (śā'ir, “poet”) + ـی (-ī).

شاگرد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شَاگِرْد (šāgird), from Middle Persian [script needed] (hšʾklt' /⁠hašāgird⁠/, “disciple, pupil”), from Proto-Iranian *hašā-kr̥táh-. Compare Bengali শাগরেদ (śagred). First attested in c. 1635 as Middle Hindi شاگِرْد (śagrd).

شال.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شال (šāl). Doublet of سَاڑِی (sāṛī). Compare Punjabi ਸ਼ਾਲ (śāl) / شال (śāl), Gujarati શાલ (śāl), Marathi शाल (śāl), Kannada ಶಾಲು (śālu), Telugu శాలువ (śāluva).

senses.g: Syria (a country in West Asia in the Middle East)
Levant (a cultural region of West Asia consisting of the eastern Mediterranean countries, i.e. Syria, Lebanon, Israel, Palestine, Jordan and Cyprus)

شام.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شام (šām, “Greater Syria; Levant”), from Arabic الشَّام (aš-šām, “Levant; Damascus”).

شامل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شامل (šāmil), from Arabic شَامِل (šāmil).

شانتی.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit शान्ति (śānti). Equivalent of شانت (śānt, “quiet”) + ـی (-ī). Doublet of چین (cain).

senses.g: inflection of شَانَہ (śāna, “shoulder; comb”): — direct plural
inflection of شَانَہ (śāna, “shoulder; comb”): — oblique/vocative singular

ex.u: شاہِ اِن٘گْلِسْتان
ex.r: śāh-i iṅglistān
ex.e: King of England

ex.u: شاہ مات!
ex.r: śāh māt!
ex.e: checkmate!

شاہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شاه (šāh), from Middle Persian 𐭬𐭫𐭪𐭠 (mlkʾ /⁠šāh⁠/) from Old Persian 𐏋 (XŠ /⁠xšāyaθiya⁠/, “king”). Compare Punjabi ਸ਼ਾਹ (śāh) / شاہ (śāh), Gujarati શાહ (śāh), Marathi शहा (śahā), Bengali শাহ (śah).

senses.g: a male given name, Shah Rukh, from Persian
Shahrug (a city in Harnai district, Balochistan, Pakistan)
a male given name from Chagatai

شاہ قلی.e: From Chagatai شاهقلی (šāh-qly, literally “slave of the Shah”); compare Azerbaijani Şahqulu, Turkish Şahkulu.

شاہ مکھی.e: Borrowed from Punjabi شاہ مکھی (śāh mukhī, “of or belonging to the royal tongue”) / ਸ਼ਾਹਮੁਖੀ (śāhmukhī) from شاہ (shāh, “king, emperor, shah”) + مکھ (mukh, “mouth”) coined after گرمکھی (gurmukhī).

senses.g: Shahnameh (an epic poem written by Ferdowsi around 1000 CE, telling the mythical and historical past of ancient Persia from the beginning of the world until the Muslim invasion)

شاہ نامہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شَاهْنَامَه (šāhnāma). By surface analysis, شاہ (śāh, “king”) + نامَہ (nāma, “history”), literally “the book of kings”.

senses.g: of or belonging to a king, royal, princely; splendid, magnificent
a dress worn by a bridegroom, a wedding-dress

شاہد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شاهد (šāhid), from Arabic شَاهِد (šāhid). Compare Punjabi شاہد / ਸ਼ਾਹਦ (śāhad).

ex.u: شاہراہ قراقرم گلگت بلتستان کی ایک اہم سڑک ہے۔
ex.r: śāhrāh qarāqaram gilgit baltistan kī ek aham saṛak hai.
ex.e: The Karakoram Highway is a strategic road in Gilgit-Baltistan.

شاہراہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شَاهْرَاه (šāhrāh). By surface analysis, شاہ (śāh) + راہ (rāh).

ex.u: شَاہِی اَنْدَاز
ex.r: śāhī andāz
ex.e: style befitting royalty

ex.u: نَوکَر شَاہِی
ex.r: naukar śāhī
ex.e: bureaucracy

senses.g: a cultivar of litchis, famously grown in Muzaffarpur
falcon, especially the peregrine falcon subspecies Falco peregrinus peregrinator.

شب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شب (šab). Cognate of Sanskrit क्षप् (kṣap).

شب بخیر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شَب بَخَیْر (šab baxayr).

senses.g: The eve of mid-Sha'ban celebrated (mostly in South Asia, Central Asia, Iran, Iraq and Turkey) in commemoration of the dead by lighting up streets and cemeteries and preparing candies and confections (especially halwas).

شب برات.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شبِ برات (šab-i-barāt), ezafe compound of شب (šab, “night”) + برات (barāt) (from Arabic بَرَاعَة (barāʕa)).

ex.u:
شَبْد شوبھا سَکھی تِرِی لوبھا
تُو سَکھی مُجْھ سے ماوْرا کَیسِی
śabd śobhā sakhī tirī lobhā
ex.r: tū sakhī mujh se māvrā kaisī
ex.e: (please add an English translation of this quotation)

senses.g: bedroom, bedchamber, a king's bed chamber
covered part of a mosque used at night
a female given name, Shabnam, from Persian

شبنم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شَبْنَم (šabnam). By surface analysis, شَب (śab, “night”) + نَم (nam, “moisture”).
Borrowed from Classical Persian شَبْنَم (šabnam). By surface analysis, شَب (śab, “night”) + نَم (nam, “moisture”).

شبہہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شبهه (šubha), from Arabic شُبْهَة (šubha), from verb شَبَّهَ (šabbaha, “to render confused”).

شتر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شتر (šutur), from Proto-Iranian *úštrah, from Proto-Indo-Iranian *úštras. Doublet of اُون٘ٹ (ū̃ṭ).

شترمرغ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شترمرغ (šuturmúrġ). Analyzable as شُتُر (śutur, “camel”) + مُرْغ (murġ, “fowl”)

ex.u:
بلندی پر انہیں مٹی کی خوشبو تک نہیں آتی
یہ وہ شاخیں ہیں جن کو اب شجر اچھا نہیں لگتا
bulandī par unhẽ miṭṭī kī xuśbū tak nahī̃ ātī
ex.r: ye vo śāxẽ haĩ jin ko ab śajar acchā nahī̃ lagtā.
ex.e:
At that lofty height, the fragrance of the soil does not reach
Those branches, to whom the tree no longer seems good.

شجر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شجر (šajar), itself from Arabic شَجَر (šajar).

شجرہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شَجَرَه (šajara), from Arabic شَجَرَة (šajara).

شخص.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شخص (šaxs), from Arabic شَخْص (šaḵṣ).

شدت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شدت (šiddat), from Arabic شِدَّة (šidda).

شدید.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شَدِید (šadīd), from Arabic شَدِيد (šadīd).

ex.u: ہِنْدوسْتانِی زُبان میں شُدّھ قُرْآن کے بولِیاں.
ex.r: hindostānī zubān mẽ śuddh qur-ān ke boliyā̃.
ex.e: Speeches of sacred Quran in Hindustani language.

شدھ.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit शुद्ध (śuddha), from the root verb शुध् (śudh, “to become pure or purified”).

شر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شر (šarr), from Arabic شَرّ (šarr).
